Section 18.6
PRINCIPLES OF NUCLEOPHILIC ADDITION: HYDRATION OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Nucleophilic Addition of Water
Water acts as a nucleophile toward ketones and aldehydes. Addition of –OH and –H across C=O is called hydration
Hydration is often very rapid; we are often interested in the equilibrium constant for this process
Is a ketone really a ketone, or is it mostly a hydrate ?
The value of K hydr is governed by intuitive structural effects

Aldehydes versus Ketones
Ketones are hydrated to a much smaller extent than aldehdyes
Inductively donating alkyl groups stabilize the carbonyl C, making hydration disfavorable

Other Electronic Effects
Electron-donating alkyl groups stabilize carbonyl C and decrease K hydr
Electron-withdrawing groups, such as perfluoroalkyl, make the carbonyl C more electrophilic and increase K hydr

Steric Effects
Large alkyl groups tend to disfavor addition
Addition brings groups from 120 degrees bond angles to smaller 109.5 degrees bond angles, increasing steric crowding

Mechanism: Base-catalyzed Hydration
Step 1: hydroxide (base) serves as a nucleophile and adds to the carbonyl carbon, forming an alkoxide intermediate
This is a classic example of nucleophilic addition to a π bond!

Mechanism: Base-catalyzed Hydration-1
Step 1: hydroxide (base) serves as a nucleophile and adds to the carbonyl carbon, forming an alkoxide intermediate
This is a classic example of nucleophilic addition to a π bond!
Step 2 : the alkoxide deprotonates water, regenerating the base

Mechanism: Acid-catalyzed Hydration
Step 1: acid protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic

Mechanism: Acid-catalyzed Hydration-1
Step 1: acid protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic
Step 2: water adds to the carbonyl carbon

Mechanism: Acid-catalyzed Hydration-2
Step 3: proton transfer from the intermediate back to water

Section 18.7
CYANOHYDRIN FORMATION

Cyanohydrin Formation
Addition of –CN and –H across C=O forms a cyanohydrin
HCN is rarely used; instead MCN is used followed by acidic workup
Mechanistically, nucleophilic addition of CN − is followed by protonation of the former carbonyl oxygen
Note the newly formed C–C bond !

Thermodynamics and Equilibrium
Trends in equilibrium constants for cyanohydrin formation mirror those for hydration to some degree
Generally, aldehydes and unhindered ketones give good yields of cyanohydrins

Section 18.8
REACTION WITH ALCOHOLS: ACETALS AND KETALS

Acetal and Ketal Formation
Nucleophilic addition of an alcohol to an aldehyde (–OR and –H across C=O ) should produce a hemiacetal

Acetal and Ketal Formation-1
Nucleophilic addition of an alcohol to an aldehyde (–OR and –H across C=O ) should produce a hemiacetal
Usually, this intermediate is not isolable—it reacts further to form an acetal
Analogous products from ketones are called ketals
Acetal formation is often thermodynamically favorable; ketal formation usually is not

Mechanism of Acetal Formation
Steps 1 – 3: acid-catalyzed addition of ROH (analogous to hydration)
From here, nucleophilic substitution of –OR for –OH occurs via an acid-promoted ( S N 1 ) mechanism

Mechanism of Acetal Formation-1
Step 4: protonation of the hydroxyl oxygen by acid
The protonated hemiacetal contains a good leaving group!

Mechanism of Acetal Formation-2
Step 5: loss of H 2 O as a leaving group
The cationic intermediate is stabilized by resonance

Mechanism of Acetal Formation-3
Step 6: nucleophilic addition by ROH to the “carbonyl” carbon
The resulting intermediate is the conjugate acid of the product
Step 7: deprotonation of the intermediate (by alcohol) to give the acetal product

Converting Acetals to Carbonyls
Excess alcohol is usually used to drive acetal formation toward products
Excess water can be used to drive this reaction back toward the carbonyl
It’s quite easy to interchange carbonyls and acetals !

Section 18.9
ACETALS AND KETALS AS PROTECTING GROUPS

Protection in Organic Synthesis
At a late stage in synthesis, we have to be careful not to destroy functional groups we’ve installed previously
For example, alkylation of an acetylide anion will not work for this transformation:
The carbonyl group reacts with strong nucleophiles! Carbonyls are base sensitive
We must convert ( protect ) the carbonyl into a functional group that does not react with bases/nucleophiles…

Acetals as Protecting Groups
Like ethers, acetals are unreactive to bases/nucleophiles
Conversion of a carbonyl to an acetal protects it from base; the acetal is called a protecting group
Reactions involving strong base are carried out when the protecting group is present …

Acetals as Protecting Groups-1
Like ethers, acetals are unreactive to bases/nucleophiles
Conversion of a carbonyl to an acetal protects it from base; the acetal is called a protecting group
Then the substrate is deprotected via hydrolysis

Section 18.10
REACTION WITH PRIMARY AMINES: IMINES

Formation of Imines
Carbonyls react with primary amines to give imines via a two-stage process:
Nucleophilic addition of –NHR and –H across R 2 C=O
Elimination of water to form R 2 C=NR
Imine formation is generally reversible, but can be driven forward by removal of water
Note that the amine must be primary

Mechanism of Imine Formation
Step 1: nucleophilic addition of amine to the carbonyl carbon

Mechanism of Imine Formation-1
Step 2: intermolecular proton transfers produce a hemiaminal intermediate

Mechanism of Imine Formation-2
Step 3: protonation of the hemiaminal oxygen
The intermediate is now poised to lose H 2 O and a proton to form the imine product

Mechanism of Imine Formation-3
Step 4: elimination of water, forming C=N
Step 5: loss of a proton from N, forming the imine
